figures.caption: Figure 4: SG/CG plane across task families. Each point is one RL recipe. deductive, abductive, and inductive near the SG = CG diagonal, but analogy in the high-SG, low-CG region.

Figure 12: Full 15-recipe per-cell marginal CG heatmaps on the 60-cell (D,T ) grid for inductive (top three rows) and analogy (bottom three rows). The layout and the colour scale match the deductive / abductive figure above, so cells can be compared across all four task families.

Figure 13: Mean depth-rank versus mean complexityrank for inductive and analogy. Each point corresponds to one RL recipe. Lower values are better on both axes. Joint-coverage recipes remain at least competitive with the best single-axis recipes.
Table 4: Composite Kinship Rules and Canonical Composition Paths
Table 3: State-Slot Semantics of Dynamic Event Classes
Figure 6: Per-task data-volume scaling. The four panels report greedy pass@1 for deductive, abductive, inductive, and analogy as the post-training data budget varies from 22k to 440k.

Figure 19: Best RL recipe per (task, depth) cell by pass@128. Rows are task families; columns are depth values D = 1, . . . , 10. Each cell is coloured by the winning recipe and labelled with the recipe name plus its pass@128 score. Deductive and abductive winners shift from Cmplx-∗ at shallow depth to Depth- or Mix-family recipes at larger depth, with absolute pass@128 dropping sharply; inductive and analogy stay in a narrow high band across depths despite changes in the winning recipe.

Figure 20: Best RL recipe per (task, complexity tier) cell by pass@128. Same convention as Figure 19 but the x-axis is the complexity tier T = 1, . . . , 6. Cmplx-family recipes appear in several low-complexity columns, while higher-complexity columns mix Depth-, Mix-, and diagonal-family winners depending on the task.

Figure 1: Two-axis data distribution and three main findings. (a) A knowledge-graph schematic shows reasoning depth and environment complexity. (b,c) Finding 1: joint depth–complexity coverage outperforms single-axis recipes. (d) Finding 2: abductive reasoning generalizes poorly outside the RL-covered data distribution. (e) Finding 3: uniform RL outperforms multi-stage curriculum under a fixed budget.

Figure 18: Cross-axis mean-rank benchmark over 22 models. Each panel ranks models by their mean performance across depth and complexity tiers for one reasoning family. Labels are shown only for models in the top-right dual-winner region.

Figure 8: Task-mix ablation under a fixed 220k budget across all four recipe families (Complex-uniform, Depthuniform, Offbase-mix, Shallow-mix). The three mixed-task settings (d140/a60, d100/a100, d60/a140) vary the deductive–abductive ratio while keeping the total budget approximately fixed.
Figure 9: Pure-task task-mix ablation under a fixed 220k budget. The figure compares pure deductive and pure abductive post-training across the four recipe families.
Table 11: Pretraining Hyperparameters
Table 13: Parsing and Matching Protocol for Generated Solutions
Table 10: Model Architecture and Tokenizer Settings
Table 12: GRPO Hyperparameters
Table 2: Notation Used in the Data Generation Framework

Figure 2: Controlled KGRL framework. The figure summarizes the synthetic KG world, four reasoning task families, and pre-training/RL post-training data distributions used to study depth–complexity coverage.

Table 9: Worked Example. Deductive and inductive are generated task instances. Abductive is constructed by masking this operation: Charity Lark Amberley exchanges the ivory chess piece with Esther Delvin Murdoch for the ebony cane. Analogy reuses the planted great-aunt path.
Table 16: RL Training Recipes over the Depth-by-Complexity Grid

Table 15: Concrete Parsing Examples for Static Reasoning Tasks. Generated solutions are copied verbatim from model outputs; parsed content is reconstructed with the same parser schema used during evaluation. Dataset identifiers are omitted.

Table 14: Concrete Parsing Examples for Dynamic Reasoning Tasks. Generated solutions are copied verbatim from model outputs; parsed content is reconstructed with the same parser schema used during evaluation. Dataset identifiers are omitted. State tuples are (owner, possessor, integrity), and both examples contain at least four target-object operations.

Table 8: Question-form inventory across the four reasoning task families. Deductive yields six forms from three query slots (possess, own, integrity) crossed with two temporal modes (full_info at Tfinal versus hard at Tq < Tfinal). Abductive yields two forms that share a single surface template but differ in the masking target. Inductive and analogy each use one question form realised through four interchangeable phrasing variants.

Figure 10: Process-reward-ratio ablation. Skill gain (SG = pass@1(RL greedy) − pass@1(Pretrain-Ep3)) as a function of process_ratio. Panels (a) Offbase-Mix and (b) Shallow-Mix show the three core task families (deductive, abductive, inductive); panel (c) compares analogy across the two recipes on a separate axis because analogy SG is much larger than the other families. Pure outcome reward (process_ratio = 0.0) hurts abductive in both recipes.

Figure 17: Pass@k curves per task family for the 15 RL recipes and the Pretrain-Ep3 baseline (black dashed). The x-axis is sample budget k on a log-2 scale. RL recipes are coloured by family (grey Baseline, blue Cmplx, green Depth, orange Diag, purple Mix), with marker variation within each family. Deductive shows consistent RL gains across k, while abductive gains are smaller and recipe-dependent; inductive shows shrinking RL advantage as k grows; analogy shows the clearest mode-collapse pattern, with most RL recipes flat near pass@k≈ 0.85 while the baseline’s pass@k rises sharply.
Table 1: Benchmark results on the 60-cell evaluation set with task- and cell-matched 3-shot prompting. Current LLMs remain weak on abductive tasks.

Figure 3: Cell-level CG heatmaps on the 60-cell (D,T ) grid. Each panel corresponds to one RL recipe; warmer colors and larger CG values indicate stronger post-training gains, while blue indicates degradation below the pre-trained model.
Table 17: Metadata for the benchmark models in Table 1.

Figure 24: Curriculum CG trajectories for inductive (left two panels) and analogy (right two panels) along the depth and complexity axes. Same layout and conventions as the deductive/abductive trajectory figure (Figure 22). The inductive and analogy trajectories show the same qualitative curriculum and replay patterns as deductive and abductive, with smaller magnitudes.

Figure 23: Full-grid CG heatmaps for the depth- and complexity-axis curricula on deductive (top two rows) and abductive (bottom two rows). Within each task block the top sub-row is the complexity-axis curriculum and the bottom sub-row is the depth-axis curriculum; the five columns are uniform, 3-block E2H, 3-block H2E, 3-block E2H +replay and many-block E2H. Cell borders are viridis-coloured by training block index (block 1 → block N, dark purple → yellow). The colour scale is shared across the two task families, and a bold dashed line separates the two task blocks.

Figure 15: Deductive–abductive correlation drill-down by recipe family. “Overall” uses all 15 recipes; each subsequent panel restricts to one recipe family and recomputes the Pearson correlation within that family. The Depth family (r = 0.62) shows the strongest within-family coupling, substantially above the overall r = 0.40; the Mix family (r = 0.17) and Baseline (r = 0.16) are weaker.

Figure 14: Remaining task-pair CG correlations across the 60-cell grid and 15 RL recipes. The deductive–analogy pair is moderately coupled, while the deductive–inductive, abductive–inductive, and abductive–analogy pairs are weakly coupled. Both axes report CG. The two strongest task pairs (deductive–abductive and inductive–analogy) are shown in the main text in Figure 5.

Figure 16: Inductive–analogy correlation drill-down by recipe family. Same layout and convention as Figure 15. The within-family pattern differs from deductive–abductive: Diag (r = 0.61) and Mix (r = 0.56) families show the strongest coupling, while the Depth family is comparatively weak (r = 0.19).

Figure 11: Full 15-recipe per-cell marginal CG heatmaps on the 60-cell (D,T ) grid for deductive (top three rows) and abductive (bottom three rows). Each task block shares the same column order: top row is the baseline plus the four Cmplx single-axis variants, middle row is Offbase-Mix plus the four Depth single-axis variants, and bottom row groups the joint-coverage, diagonal and full-coverage recipes. Black borders mark each recipe’s post-training cells, green borders mark the pre-training cells (D≤4, T≤2), and grey cells were not evaluated. The colour scale is shared with the inductive / analogy figure below.

Figure 22: Curriculum CG trajectories for deductive and abductive on the depth and complexity axes. Within each task pair, the left panel shows depth schedules and the right panel shows complexity schedules. Higher curves indicate stronger CG.

Figure 21: Best RL recipe per (task, region) cell by pass@128. The nine regions partition the 60-cell grid into depth bands (D ∈ [1, 4], [5, 7], [8, 10]) crossed with complexity bands (T ∈ [1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]). Same convention as Figures 19 and 20.
